turns.text: Hello Ethel, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you for asking. Honestly, it’s hard to know where to even start. My head has been pounding for days—a constant, thrumming reminder that my body isn't quite doing what it’s supposed to. 

This pregnancy was supposed to be the "familiar path," you know? But everything feels heavy now, literally and figuratively. Driving in from Rowley, every mile just makes me think about the shifts I’m missing at work and how the bills are piling up while I’m sitting in these cold waiting rooms. I feel like I’m walking toward a financial cliff, and I’m terrified we’re going to fall right off it.

Then there’s the "high-risk" label. Every time a nurse looks at my chart, I see that look—that flicker of pity. It makes me feel less like a person and more like a complicated math equation they’re trying to balance. I just want someone to look at *me*, not just the blood pressure monitor or the insurance paperwork. I spend my nights staring at the ceiling, wondering if the magnesium or the stress is going to ruin that first moment with my twins. I just need to know my body can hold on long enough to get them here safely. It’s all just… it’s a lot to carry.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

To be honest, I didn't even bother writing one down. I know people talk about birth plans like they’re some golden ticket to how things should go, but after they diagnosed the pre-eclampsia, that felt like a fantasy. How do you plan for a "dream birth" when your own blood pressure is essentially a ticking time bomb? 

I felt like if I walked in with a list of demands, the staff would just look at me like I was delusional. When you're marked as "high-risk" and your chart is full of numbers they’re worried about, you stop feeling like you have a voice. I was terrified that if I asked for things—like dim lights or music or a specific way to move—they’d just dismiss me or think I wasn't taking the medical side seriously. 

In the end, there was no plan. The reality of it was just survival. Everything happened so fast, and once the monitors were on and the magnesium started, I wasn't the one steering the ship anymore. I just had to lie there, hooked up to all those machines, hoping they knew what they were doing. I felt like a passenger in my own delivery, watching from the sidelines while my body was managed like a broken machine. It wasn't about what I wanted; it was about whether we’d make it through at all.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I think I naively expected a conversation. You see all those brochures in the clinic waiting room that talk about "empowered birth" and "shared decision-making." I thought, maybe if I could just get a doctor to really look me in the eye, we could talk about what I was scared of, how I handle pain, and what my limits were. I wanted to feel like I had a seat at the table.

But the reality? It wasn’t a choice; it was an intervention. Because of the pre-eclampsia, the "pain management" conversation was pretty much decided for me before I even walked in the door. There wasn't a menu of options to pick from—there was the protocol. Everything felt urgent, like my labor was just a series of fires they had to put out. I remember feeling so frantic, and when the pain started peaking, I felt like the staff was looking at me through the lens of my blood pressure numbers rather than my actual physical experience. 

They didn't really support me in *deciding* anything. They told me what was necessary to keep the twins safe, and who can argue with that? It felt like the machines were the ones in charge, and I was just the container. I didn't feel supported; I felt processed.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

When I pulled into the hospital parking lot, my heart was already pounding against my ribs. I kept telling myself that this was the safest place to be, that the machines and the specialists were there to protect the babies from whatever my body was doing wrong. I wanted to believe it would be a place of care—a sanctuary where I could finally breathe, knowing that help was right there. I wanted to feel like I was being welcomed into a partnership.

The reality was much colder. From the moment I walked through those sliding doors, I felt like a statistic, not a mother. The environment was loud, sterile, and blindingly bright. It didn't feel like a space made for bringing life into the world; it felt like an emergency department. 

Every time a nurse entered the room, it felt like an intrusion. They weren't there to ask how I was holding up or to offer a kind word; they were there to check the monitor, silence an alarm, or adjust a drip. I felt like a burden in their busy schedule, and that look of pity I dreaded was always there. I kept waiting for someone to just hold my hand and tell me, "We’ve got this together," but instead, I felt like a problem they were trying to contain. I didn't feel safe; I just felt exposed.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise? It was how incredibly *lonely* it felt, even with four or five people in the room at any given time. I had expected the hospital to be this hive of activity where I’d be surrounded by support, like you see in movies where everyone is cheering or guiding you through the process. Instead, the atmosphere was just… mechanical.

It was the noise that really got to me. It wasn’t the sounds of birth; it was the constant, rhythmic beeping of the monitors. That high-pitched, persistent electronic pulse seemed to be the only thing the staff cared about. Every time an alarm chirped, the room would tense up. I’d see them glance at the screen, adjust a dial, and then move on to the next task without even meeting my eyes. 

I was physically in a room with people, but I felt like I was drifting in the middle of an ocean alone. The silence in the room wasn't peaceful—it was a heavy, suffocating kind of quiet where my own fears had nowhere to go. I realized then that "clinical safety" is a very different thing from feeling human. I was in the safest building in the state, yet I had never felt more invisible.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I knew my pregnancy was "high-risk," so I expected that if things went sideways, there would be a pause. I thought there would be a moment where the doctor would sit down, look me in the eye, and explain, "Ethel, this is happening, here is why we need to do this, and here are the risks." I expected a conversation, even if it was a quick one. I thought I would at least have the dignity of being asked for my consent.

But when the interventions hit, the "conversation" vanished entirely. There were no explanations, just commands. It was like I’d suddenly become an object on the table rather than a participant in my own life. A nurse would announce, "We're starting this," or "You need this now," and before I could even process the words, a needle was in my arm or a catheter was being inserted. 

It was all communicated with this frantic, detached urgency. They didn't see a woman who was terrified; they saw a set of vitals they had to stabilize. I wasn't involved in the decision-making; I was just being told what was being done to me. It made me feel so small, like my own voice was just background noise to the alarms.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

There was no "choice" in those moments—it was all strictly "happened to me." 

When the blood pressure started spiking and the babies' heart rates dipped, the room changed. The air felt thin, and the humanity just sort of drained out of the space. Every action they took felt like a reflex, not a decision we were making together. I didn't get information; I got directives. It was like, "We’re doing this because the numbers say so," and that was the end of it. 

I kept trying to catch someone’s eye, trying to ask *why* or *what next*, but they were all looking at the screens. It was like I was a piece of equipment that needed a quick fix to keep running. I felt completely stripped of my agency. Even though I knew they were technically doing their jobs—trying to keep the three of us alive—it’s a different thing to be a person and a totally different thing to be a patient whose body is being managed by a team that’s too rushed to explain the "why." 

I wasn't a mother in that room; I was a crisis that needed to be resolved. It left me feeling so hollow, like I’d been stripped of my own birth story before it even truly began.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I think I expected—or maybe just desperately hoped for—someone to be an anchor. You know, someone who would recognize that behind the pre-eclampsia and the terrifying numbers on the monitor, there was a woman who was scared to death. I wanted a hand to hold, or just someone to look at me and say, "Ethel, I know you're terrified, but we're going to get you through this." I wanted them to see the mother, not just the case study.

But the reality was a complete void of that kind of support. The staff were professional, I suppose, if you define professional as "efficient," but they were emotionally miles away. It felt like they were operating on autopilot, fueled by duty and protocol, not compassion. If I cried out or if my fear showed on my face, it was ignored or met with a quick, clinical reassurance that felt hollow—like a script they were reading off a wall. 

They didn't give me attention; they gave me interventions. They were present in the room, but they weren't *with* me. I was surrounded by people, yet I was entirely alone in my fear. It’s hard to put into words how much that absence of kindness hurt, maybe even more than the physical pain of it all.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

There was a moment, near the very end, that still haunts me. I was shaking from the magnesium—my whole body was vibrating, and I felt so incredibly cold and small. I remember looking at a nurse who was adjusting one of the drips. I was trying to ask her if the babies were okay, or maybe just begging her to tell me it was almost over, but my voice was weak and it didn't come out right. 

She didn’t even look down at my face. She just kept her eyes glued to the pump, adjusting the dial with this practiced, mechanical flick of her wrist, and said, "Just breathe, honey. Your numbers are still high."

That was it. That was the moment I felt truly, utterly alone. She called me "honey," but there wasn’t a shred of warmth in it; it was just a label, a way to dismiss the fact that I was a human being screaming for connection inside a body that felt like it was betraying me. In that second, the wall between the "clinical" and the "personal" felt like a chasm I couldn't cross. I wasn't Ethel, the mother of these two babies. I was just a collection of readings on a chart that needed to be stabilized before she could move on to her next patient. I’ve never felt more invisible.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I had this beautiful, quiet image in my head. I imagined that moment of chaos ending and everything suddenly going still. I wanted to hear them cry, to have them placed right on my chest—warm, slippery, and real. I wanted to finally hold them and let them know we’d made it through the storm together. I expected to feel that overwhelming rush of relief and love, with my husband right there, just us, finally being a family.

But that’s not what happened. It was a blur of bright lights and sharp, efficient movement. Because of the twins and the complications, there were so many people in the room—doctors, nurses, specialists, all crowding around. The moment they arrived, it felt like they were whisked away to another part of the room, surrounded by equipment and humming machines. 

I was still shaking, pinned down by the monitors, feeling so weak and drugged from the medications. I couldn't reach out; I could barely even see them clearly. There was no skin-to-skin. There was no quiet "we did it." Instead, it was a flurry of medical activity—assessments, vitals, weighing—and I was left there on the table, still tethered to the machines, just a spectator to my own children’s first breaths. I wasn't their mother in that room; I was just someone recovering from a procedure. It felt like I was being robbed of the most important first step.
Was there anything that happened — or didn't happen — in those first moments that still stays with you? What was it?

What stays with me—and it’s the thing that wakes me up in the middle of the night—is the silence of my own arms. 

I kept expecting to feel that weight against my skin. Even after they were born, I had this hollow space in front of me where they should have been. I could hear them, I could hear the nurses talking about APGAR scores and oxygen levels, but I couldn't feel them. The medical team had completely taken over the space around me. They were working so hard to "manage" the babies that they acted like I wasn't even there. 

There was a second where one of the twins was being moved past me, and I reached out, just a tiny bit, hoping someone would tuck them against me for even a second. But the nurse just said, "Not yet, Mom, we need to monitor their breathing first." And then they were gone, behind a curtain of equipment and backs. 

It’s that feeling of being an afterthought that sticks. I’d spent months terrified for their lives, agonizing over every ultrasound, just to survive the delivery and then be sidelined while they were being cared for by everyone but me. I felt like a stranger to my own children in those first, precious minutes. I didn't get to welcome them; I just got to watch them being handled by strangers.
Before giving birth, what did you expect your physical recovery to look and feel like in the weeks after? And how did the reality of your physical recovery compare to those expectations?

I suppose I expected the "normal" exhaustion people talk about—the late nights, the diaper changes, just being tired because I’m a new mom of twins. I thought there would be a focus on healing from the birth itself, maybe some soreness, but that I’d eventually find a rhythm. I thought I’d be able to just be a mother, focusing on nursing and getting to know their little faces.

The reality hit me like a train. It wasn't just the physical exhaustion; it was the hangover from the pre-eclampsia. My body felt like it had been through a war. My blood pressure was still a nightmare, so instead of focusing on the babies, I was constantly checking my own pulse, wondering if I was going to stroke out or have a seizure. I felt physically fragile, like a piece of glass that had been dropped.

And the anxiety—that didn't leave. Every time I looked at the twins, I was haunted by that hospital room. I felt so depleted, both from the blood loss and the sheer emotional trauma of feeling like an outsider in my own birth. I had to go back and forth for follow-ups, and every time I saw that hospital sign, my heart started racing. I didn't get a recovery period; I got a continuation of the crisis. I was just trying to survive the day while my body struggled to remember how to be healthy again. It wasn't the "motherhood" I’d dreamt about; it was a long, slow climb out of a very deep hole.

Before your baby arrived, what did you expect feeding — breastfeeding or formula feeding — to be like in terms of ease, support, and how you'd feel about it? And what was the reality of feeding in the early weeks?

I thought feeding would be the one thing that was just... natural. You know, you see all the pamphlets about the "golden hour" and the bond that comes with nursing. I really wanted that. I wanted those quiet, skin-to-skin moments where I’d finally feel like a mom, not a patient. I expected it might be tricky with twins—double the work, right?—but I figured there would be someone there to sit with me, help me get the latch, and just encourage me when I got frustrated.

The reality was such a lonely, jagged experience. Because of the pre-eclampsia, my recovery was so slow and the drugs they had me on left me feeling like a zombie. My milk didn't come in right away because my body was so stressed out, and every time I tried to nurse, I was shaking, either from the residual magnesium or just plain panic. 

I didn't get help; I got a handout of formula and a, "Let us know if you need anything." Every time I tried to nurse, I felt like I was failing them, and the nurses were just watching, waiting for me to ask for a bottle. It felt less like a choice and more like a surrender. I wanted so badly to be their source of comfort, but I just felt like I was failing at the one thing I thought I could control. My body had already let them down during the birth; not being able to feed them easily felt like the final blow.
Was there a gap between the kind of feeding support you were offered and the kind of support you actually needed?

Oh, definitely. The support I was offered felt like a checklist, not actual care. Someone would come in, maybe spend two minutes adjusting a pillow or saying something like, “It takes time, just keep trying,” and then they were out the door before I could even find the words to explain that I felt like I was physically falling apart.

I didn’t need a pamphlet or a quick, generic pep talk; I needed someone to sit with me while I was terrified of my own body. I was so anxious about my blood pressure and the medication I was still on that every time I held them, I was convinced I was going to drop them or pass out. I needed someone to hold *me* up while I tried to hold them. 

Instead, the staff seemed to treat feeding as something that either happened or it didn't, and if it was hard, well, that’s just how it goes. I felt like a failure because my body wasn’t cooperating, and the lack of patient, gentle, hands-on help just made that isolation even worse. They offered "advice," but I needed *presence*. I needed someone to acknowledge that I was struggling—not just with the latch, but with the trauma of those first days in the hospital. Instead, I just felt like I was on my own, stumbling through a dark room.
Before your baby arrived, what did you expect your emotional and mental health to be like in the postpartum period? And what was your actual emotional experience in the weeks and months after birth?

Before the birth, I had this naive idea that once the babies were here, the "high-risk" label would drop off like a heavy coat, and I’d finally be able to breathe. I expected to be tired, sure—I knew that—but I thought I’d feel this overwhelming sense of peace. I expected my heart would just expand to fit the two of them. I thought I’d be able to look at them and feel that magic, that "everything is going to be okay" feeling.

The reality was, and still is, so far from that. The trauma didn't leave when the hospital monitors were unplugged. Every time I hear a beeping sound—even a microwave or a phone—I’m instantly back in that room, feeling my chest constrict. I’m living in a state of hyper-vigilance, constantly checking the babies, checking my own blood pressure, terrified that if I relax, something else will go wrong. 

It’s like I’m walking through a fog. I feel so detached sometimes, and then other times I’m just sobbing, not because I’m sad, but because I’m grieving the birth I didn't get to have. I wanted to feel powerful, like a mother who brought life into the world, but instead, I feel like a survivor of a medical emergency. The postpartum period hasn't been a time of recovery; it's been a long, quiet fight to feel like a human again.
Was there a gap between what you were feeling and what you felt you were 'allowed' to feel — by professionals, family, or yourself?

Oh, there’s a massive gap. Everyone tells you, "The babies are healthy! You all made it, that's what matters." And while, yes, I’m grateful for that—so deeply grateful—that statement acts like a lid they’re trying to press down on everything I'm actually feeling. If I bring up how traumatized I am, or how much I hate that I couldn’t hold them right away, I feel like I’m being ungrateful. It’s like I’m not "allowed" to grieve the birth because the outcome was "success."

Professionals are the worst about this. Their version of success is alive babies and stable blood pressure. My version of success—actually being *present* and having a moment to connect—is just an afterthought to them. When I try to talk about the fear, they look at my chart and just say, "Well, you're cleared for discharge."

And family? They just see the twins and think I should be glowing. How do I tell them that I’m vibrating with the ghost of that magnesium drip? I feel like I have to wear a mask of "the happy, relieved mother" all day, every day. It’s exhausting. It’s like being a performer in a play where the script says I should be joyful, but I’m actually just waiting for the next catastrophe to strike.
Before becoming a parent, what did you expect your sense of identity to feel like after your baby arrived? And how has your sense of who you are actually shifted since then?

Before the twins, I had this idea that I was just evolving. I was Ethel, a wife, a worker, a woman navigating life in Rowley. I thought motherhood would just be another layer, like putting on a coat—it would fit, it would be warm, and I’d still be *me*. I wanted to be a partner to my husband and a solid, steady person for my children.

But now? That identity feels like it was put through a shredder. 

Between the pre-eclampsia, the constant financial stress of missing work, and the way the hospital system handled me, I don’t recognize myself anymore. I’m not a woman anymore; I’m a "high-risk patient" or a "mother of multiples." Every conversation is about the babies' sleep, the cost of diapers, or my blood pressure levels. My identity has been swallowed up by the survival mode I’ve been living in since the day I found out I was having twins. 

I feel like a ghost of who I was. I don't feel "empowered," which is what the books promised. I feel like a vessel that was used and then left to clean up the mess. I’m trying to find "Ethel" again, but she’s buried under a mountain of medical anxiety and the heavy, constant pressure to keep everyone else safe. It's like I’ve lost my own edges.
Were there parts of your previous identity — professional, social, personal — that felt harder to hold onto than you expected?

Everything felt hard to hold onto, but my identity as a worker—the part of me that felt capable and productive—that one felt the most fragile. I was used to being the one who shows up for shifts, who manages her own life, who doesn’t need to ask for help unless it’s absolutely necessary. But once the pre-eclampsia took hold and I was in and out of the hospital, I had to stop working. 

Watching our bank account drain while I couldn't do a thing about it—that really crushed me. I felt like I went from being a provider to being a "cost" or a liability. That was such a sharp, painful shift. Socially, I became a ghost. I didn't want to see friends, because what would I even say? "Hi, I’m traumatized, I’m broke, and I’m scared to hold my own children"? It felt like nobody really *got* it. They’d look at the twins and talk about how lucky I was, and I’d just smile and feel like I was wearing a costume.

Even the simple things, like being a wife to my husband, felt like they were slipping away. We were both just terrified and exhausted, constantly checking the monitors or the bills. I missed being a person who could just *be* without being on high alert. That part of me—the one who wasn’t just a patient or a mother—is the piece I miss the most.
After birth, what did you expect the postnatal check-ups and follow-up care from your midwife or GP to provide? And what did that post-birth care actually look like in practice?

I expected those check-ups to be a place to finally process everything. I thought, *Okay, now that the "emergency" is over and the babies are home, someone is going to sit me down and walk me through what actually happened.* I wanted them to look at the chart with me, explain the pre-eclampsia, and, more importantly, ask how I was *doing*—not just how my blood pressure was, but if I was having nightmares or if the anxiety was making it impossible to sleep. I expected a bridge between the trauma of the hospital and my life as a mom.

The reality? It was a conveyor belt. I’d walk into the clinic, and it was the same sterile environment, the same hurried energy. The check-ups lasted ten, maybe fifteen minutes. They’d take my vitals, write something on a clipboard, and ask if I was "feeling okay." I’d try to say something like, "I’m really struggling with the memories of the birth," and they’d just nod, write down "anxiety," and hand me a flyer for a support group or a prescription refill. 

It never felt like a conversation; it felt like data collection. They were checking to make sure I wasn't going to have a stroke, which I get, but they didn't seem to care if I was actually *living*. I walked out of every appointment feeling more alone than when I walked in. It was like they treated my physical recovery as a success story while completely ignoring the fact that my mind was still trapped back in that delivery room.
Were there concerns you raised at postnatal appointments that you felt were not taken seriously or properly followed up?

Every time I tried to mention how I wasn't sleeping—how every time I closed my eyes I could still hear that monitor beeping or feel the cold, stinging burn of the IV—they’d just look down at my chart. They’d see that my blood pressure had finally stabilized, and they’d smile, like, "Great news, Ethel! You're doing fine." 

"Fine." That’s the word they kept using. I’d try to explain, "I’m not 'fine,' I’m terrified all the time," and they’d just pivot back to my numbers. It was as if my emotional health was secondary to their clinical metrics. They’d ask if I was "bonding" with the babies, and I’d have to lie and say yes because I was too ashamed to tell them that I felt like I was holding two strangers I’d been forced to deliver in a war zone.

I wanted them to ask me about the *birth*—to acknowledge that it wasn't just a "medical procedure" that happened to a vessel, but a traumatic event that happened to a woman. Instead, they just marked "postpartum anxiety" on a form and sent me on my way. It felt like I was being told, "You're alive, the babies are alive, what else could you possibly want?" I wanted to be *heard*. I wanted to feel like I mattered as more than just a successful outcome.
If you returned to work after your baby was born, what did you expect that transition to be like — practically and emotionally? And what did returning to work actually feel like?

The return to work was a nightmare I wasn't prepared for. I had this idea that going back would be a relief, that it would give me back a piece of my old self—Ethel the worker, the one who does a job, gets paid, and contributes. I thought it would be a break from the constant, suffocating focus on the babies and the trauma of the birth.

But in reality, it felt like I was splitting into two people. When I’m at work, I’m constantly looking at the clock, my heart jumping every time my phone vibrates, convinced that something has gone wrong at home. I’m physically present, but my mind is still in that hospital room, still waiting for an alarm to go off. My body feels tired—bone-deep, soul-sucking tired—and the financial stress doesn’t just go away because I’m earning again. It’s like the bills are a monster that’s still chasing me.

And the emotional whiplash is constant. I go from the pressure of my shift to the immense, terrifying responsibility of two infants, and I never actually get to exhale. I’m not the woman I was before; I’m someone who’s just trying to keep all the plates spinning, terrified that if I drop one, everything will shatter. I’m not really "back" at work; I’m just surviving there, too.

After your baby arrived, what did you expect your relationship with your partner to be like in terms of sharing caregiving, managing the relationship, and supporting each other? And what has that relationship dynamic actually looked like?

I think I expected us to be a team. You know, "us against the world." I imagined us navigating the sleepless nights together, laughing when we were exhausted, and leaning on each other when the weight of being new parents to twins felt like too much. I thought we’d talk—really talk—about how the birth had changed us, and that we’d hold space for each other’s fears.

The reality has been more like us just trying to keep our heads above water in separate drowning pools. It’s not that he isn't trying, but the stress has just... hardened us. When you’re constantly worried about money, and then you add two babies with high-risk health concerns, there’s no room left for "us." We don’t talk; we trade off. He’s working, I’m working, and we’re both just perpetually behind. 

It feels like we’re two people standing in a house that’s burning down, each trying to rescue one child and barely looking at the other. We’re both so depleted that when we finally do have a second to breathe, we’re just too tired to be tender. We’re not partners in a shared life anymore; we’re co-managers of a crisis. I hate that it’s this way, but when you're just trying to survive, you don't have the luxury of being a couple.
What was the biggest surprise about how your partnership functioned after the baby arrived?

The biggest surprise—and honestly, the one that hurts the most—was how *quiet* we’ve become. Before the babies, we could talk about everything. We had plans, we had dreams, and we were always on the same wavelength. Now, it’s like there’s this unspoken wall between us built out of pure, raw exhaustion. 

I think we both realized that we were each carrying a different kind of trauma. He saw me on that hospital bed, hooked up to the magnesium and the monitors, watching me shake and hearing the alarms, and he felt utterly helpless. I think that helplessness turned into a kind of protective shell for him, where he just goes into "fix it" mode—doing the chores, paying the bills, keeping the babies alive—because he’s terrified of what would happen if he actually stopped to process how close we came to losing everything.

We don't talk about the birth because we both know that if we open that door, the floodgates will open, and we’re just too tired to handle that much pain. So, we trade shifts in silence. We hand the babies off like relay batons. It’s like we’re two strangers living in the same house, trying to survive a storm that we’re both too afraid to name. It’s not the life I imagined, but it’s the life that keeps us upright.

Looking across your entire maternity journey — from trying to conceive through to where you are now — what did you expect the overall experience of maternity care to deliver? And reflecting on the whole journey, what was the most significant gap between what you expected and what you received?

I expected maternity care to be a bridge. I thought it would be a compassionate, guided experience that helped me navigate the fears of being high-risk and the physical toll of carrying twins. I wanted to feel like I was walking a path with experts who were invested in the "mother" just as much as they were in the "pregnancy." I expected to be treated like a person, to be listened to, and to be empowered to participate in my own story, even if that story had to be a medical one.

The most significant gap was the total absence of humanity in the face of protocol. They were so focused on "clinical success"—my blood pressure numbers and the babies' survival—that they completely abandoned the woman having the experience. I was treated like an object to be managed, a set of complications to be resolved. 

They gave me medical safety, sure, and I’m alive to talk about it—but they took away my dignity, my agency, and my peace of mind. I didn't need to be handled; I needed to be heard. I needed someone to acknowledge that behind the screen of the monitors, there was a scared woman who was losing her sense of self. That gap—between "surviving" and "being cared for"—is the hole I’m still trying to climb out of every single day.
If you could change one thing about how the maternity care system is designed — not just what individual providers do, but how the system itself works — what would it be?

If I could change one thing, it would be the way the system compartmentalizes everything. Right now, it’s all silos—the obstetrician focuses on the blood pressure, the nurses focus on the monitor, and the billing department focuses on the insurance. There is absolutely no space for the *person* who is caught in the middle of it all. 

I would design a system where "continuity of care" isn't just a buzzword in a brochure, but a real, human connection. Every high-risk pregnancy should have a dedicated advocate whose only job is to be the bridge between the medical "protocol" and the patient’s reality. Someone whose primary responsibility isn't to look at a monitor, but to look at *me*, to explain what is happening in language that isn't terrifying, and to make sure I’m not lost in the shuffle of clinical directives.

The system needs to realize that a birth isn't just a physical procedure you survive; it's an experience that marks you for the rest of your life. If the system was built to prioritize the mother's mental and emotional stability *alongside* the medical data, maybe I wouldn't feel like I was left to rebuild myself from scratch. I want a system that sees the mother as a participant in her own story, not as an obstacle to the medical outcome.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, the thing that sticks in my throat when I think about it is how we’re expected to just "bounce back." Society—and the doctors, too—have this narrative that because my babies are here and I’m technically healthy, the story is over. It’s like, "Congratulations, here’s your life back, go on with it."

But no one talks about the fact that I’m still mourning the version of myself that existed before that hospital room. I feel like I’m constantly performing "motherhood" while inside I’m still trembling, still waiting for an alarm to scream, still feeling that cold, detached hand on my shoulder. 

I wish someone had told me that it’s okay to be both profoundly grateful for my twins and deeply angry at how I was treated. I’m not ungrateful—I love them more than I can say—but I’m also grieving. I’m grieving the loss of my agency, the loss of those first hours, and the loss of the sense of safety in my own skin. I think if the system could just acknowledge that *both* can be true at once—that you can be a happy mom and a traumatized patient—it would change everything for women like me. We aren't just "success stories"; we are people who have been through something that changes us forever. Thank you for listening. Really. It’s the first time in a long time I’ve felt like my voice actually mattered.
